text: The surgeon uses the HD-III-D enhanced visualization of the robotic platform to carefully identify and separate the tissue planes between the fascia and the adherent small bowel, addressing difficult and potentially dangerous adhesions.
The surgeon uses sharp scissors without electrocautery to carefully dissect tissue, taking advantage of the articulating wristed instruments to avoid injury to the intestines.

The surgeon uses a Cartier grasper in the left hand, visible on the left side of the screen, to gently manipulate the intestines without causing trauma. As the dissection moves away from the intestines, electrocautery is applied to separate the more vascular fibro fatty adhesions.

The surgeon performs a primary closure of the incisional hernia using a V-lock polypropylene suture. The barbed suture is secured to the fascia without knotting, and the end is doubled back to enhance repair strength and efficiency.
The surgeon begins the primary repair by placing a figure-of-eight suture and pulling it through, securing it to itself using the loop at the end of the V-lock polypropylene suture.
The surgeon uses a long suture to place several throws through the fascia, then begins tightening the suture line by pulling it through the muscle, resulting in visible suture manipulation.

Carbon dioxide is released to reduce intra-abdominal pressure, allowing the fascial edges of a large defect to be approximated. The reduced pressure results in a loss of ceiling space, making instrument manipulation challenging.

The surgeon places a portion of the suture line and releases intra-abdominal pressure to tighten the suture line. After re-insufflation, the surgeon continues suturing, approximating the fascial edges effectively.

The surgeon uses mobile needle holders to facilitate suturing, passing the needle between the right and left hands for optimal angles. Multiple suture passes are placed, and the suture line is tightened to pull the muscle edges together. Carbon dioxide pressure is released to allow the edges to approximate.

The surgeon focuses on the inferior margin of the defect, working superiorly towards the initial suture line. The suture line is being tightened from the bottom, with visible external compression applied to the abdominal wall by the assistant to aid in approximating the muscle edges.

The surgeon completes the suture line by crossing over the superior and inferior suture lines. The V-lock suture maintains tension effectively without a knot. The needle is removed and placed in the peritoneum to prevent misplacement, followed by tying a knot between the suture ends.

The surgeon places a mesh over the primary repair for reinforcement. A purling suture is secured to the superior and inferior portions of the mesh. The suture tails are brought out through the abdominal wall using a suture passer, and tied in the subcutaneous space for trans-fascial fixation. The bedside assistant is seen placing the suture passer through the abdominal wall.

The proline sutures are used to hold the mesh against the underside of the fascia. The bedside assistant places absorbable tacks around the mesh's circumference while the primary surgeon holds the mesh in place and indicates the tack placement locations. The mesh can alternatively be sewn in place using robotic suturing techniques.
The surgical team completes the repair of the incisional hernia, finalizing the closure with the mesh securely in place.
